all_words_learned.casual_usage: Used in plain form in casual speech — 「どこいくの？」(Doko iku no?) means 'Where are you going?' You'll hear this constantly among friends.
all_words_learned.formal_usage: Becomes 「いきます」(ikimasu) in polite form. 「どこへいきますか？」(Doko e ikimasu ka?) is the formal equivalent for acquaintances or work.

similar_words.translation: to come
to return / go home
to head out / go out

all_words_learned.translation: when you're not hungry, but you eat because your mouth is lonely

all_words_learned.notes: (also said "Ku" - but avoided since it can mean suffering)
most common in everyday speech. From English. Used for phone numbers, scores, temperatures

all_words_learned.casual_usage: Dropped straight into casual questions — 「いつくるの？」(Itsu kuru no?) means 'When are you coming?' It pairs naturally with verbs like いく and くる that you already know.
all_words_learned.formal_usage: いつ itself never changes form; only the verb after it shifts — 「いつきますか？」(Itsu kimasu ka?) is the polite equivalent.

similar_words.translation: where
who
what

all_words_learned.casual_usage: いま is everywhere in casual speech — 「いまなに？」(ima nani?) means 'What are you up to right now?' and 「いまいい？」(ima ii?) is the quick way to ask 'Is now a good time?' It drops naturally in front of almost any verb.
all_words_learned.formal_usage: The word itself doesn't change in formal settings — 「いまよろしいですか？」(ima yoroshii desu ka?) is the polite way to ask 'Is now okay?' Only the verb that follows shifts into ます/です form.

similar_words.translation: already / now (signals something has changed)
right away / immediately
later

all_words_learned.what_it_is: いただきます is the expression Japanese people say — hands together, often eyes closed — before every single meal. Literally meaning 'I humbly receive,' it's directed at the food itself, the animals and plants that became it, the farmers who grew it, and the person who cooked it. There's no English equivalent because English-speaking cultures simply don't have this ritual baked into daily life.

similar_words.translation: thanks for the feast (said after eating)
thanks to your support (humble gratitude)
what a waste (the feeling of guilt over wasting something precious)

user_words.translation: when you're not hungry, but you eat because your mouth is lonely

user_words.notes: (also said "Ku" - but avoided since it can mean suffering)
most common in everyday speech. From English. Used for phone numbers, scores, temperatures

daily_words.what_it_is: 木漏れ日 names something everyone has seen but few languages bother to name outright: the dappled, shifting light that falls through a canopy of leaves. It bundles the sunlight, the leaves, and the shadows into a single word, so instead of describing a scene you just point at it with one breath. It's one of the words most often cited by Japanese speakers as 'impossible to translate' — English needs a whole clause to say what this says in three syllables.

similar_words.translation: the shade cast by a tree
a warm, sunny patch (often a cozy spot to sit)
a gentle, rustling breeze

daily_words.example_context: It surfaces on a forest walk in early summer, when you look down and notice the ground is covered in shifting circles of light instead of describing the scene piece by piece.
daily_words.bonus: Komorebi shows up constantly in Studio Ghibli-style visual language and is a favorite word among translators precisely because it can't be rendered in one English word — it's frequently listed alongside 'hygge' and 'saudade' in articles about untranslatable feelings.

daily_words.translation: buying books and letting them pile up unread
daily_words.what_it_is: 積読 fuses 積む (tsumu, to pile up) with 読 (doku, reading), with a sly pun buried inside on 置く (oku, to leave lying around). It names the very specific, slightly guilty habit of buying more books than you'll ever get through, letting them stack up on shelves and nightstands as promises to a future self. Rather than shaming the habit, Japanese gave it a name — which somehow makes it feel like a legitimate hobby instead of a character flaw.

similar_words.translation: decluttering — discarding excess to live simpler
reading (the earnest, guilt-free opposite of tsundoku)
what a waste (the guilt of leaving something precious unused)

daily_words.example_context: It surfaces the moment you eye a nightstand stack of five unread books while checking out with a sixth.
daily_words.bonus: Tsundoku dates back to satirical wordplay in the 1870s Meiji era, mocking teachers who bought books to look scholarly without ever reading them — today it's been happily reclaimed worldwide by book lovers as the perfect label for their overflowing shelves.

daily_words.translation: the fluttery, bubbling excitement of anticipation
daily_words.what_it_is: わくわく is a gitaigo — a word that mimics a feeling rather than a sound — for the bubbly, can't-sit-still excitement of looking forward to something good. The doubled syllable mirrors the sensation itself, like a little repeating pulse of anticipation building in your chest. It's one of the first mimetic words Japanese kids learn, precisely because it captures a universal feeling English has to reach for phrases like 'giddy with excitement' to describe.

similar_words.translation: the pounding-heart feeling of nervousness or excitement
restless, fidgety anticipation
a light, bouncy, cheerful mood

daily_words.example_context: It surfaces the night before a trip, a festival, or opening a package you've been waiting for, when you physically can't sit still.
daily_words.bonus: Wakuwaku is so ingrained in pop culture that it shows up in anime, game titles, and even Kirby's Japanese branding — the doubled-syllable pattern is a hallmark of gitaigo, letting a single repeated sound stand in for a whole paragraph of description.

daily_words.translation: the person or character you passionately support / your "fave"
daily_words.what_it_is: 推し comes from the verb 推す (osu), 'to push' or 'to endorse' — literally, the one you're pushing for. It's bigger than 'favorite': it names the object of devoted, semi-parasocial cheerleading — an idol, an anime character, a K-pop member, even an athlete — whose happiness and success you root for like a personal mission. The word has spawned its own vocabulary and economy, because English has no single word for 'the person I've organized part of my emotional life around supporting.'

similar_words.translation: the hobby of actively supporting your oshi (merch, events, streams)
'precious' — fan slang for something so moving or cute it hurts
the hardcore, dead-serious crowd within a fandom

daily_words.example_context: It surfaces when a friend asks who your favorite idol or character is, and you end up giving a five-minute speech about why they deserve the world.
daily_words.bonus: The term exploded internationally after the hit manga/anime 'Oshi no Ko' (2023), and 推し活 has been named among Japan's buzzwords of the year — companies now sell dedicated 推し活 planners and stationery just for organizing your devotion.

daily_words.translation: the soft, plush fluffiness of fur, feathers, or stuffed animals
daily_words.what_it_is: もふもふ is a gitaigo that doesn't describe a sound at all — it conjures a texture, the kind of dense, cushiony fluffiness you want to bury your hands in. Say it out loud and the repeated 'mo' sounds practically feel soft, which is the whole magic trick of Japanese mimetic words: sound standing in for sensation. It's the word every dog-and-cat account on Japanese social media reaches for first.

similar_words.translation: light and airy softness (like a cloud or fresh pancake)
puffy, cloud-like poofiness (a fluffy sweater or clouds)
smooth and slippery (the opposite texture feeling)

daily_words.example_context: It surfaces the second someone posts a photo of their dog fresh out of a bath, or a Pomeranian mid-fluff, captioned with nothing but もふもふ.
daily_words.bonus: Mofumofu is a cornerstone of Japan's kawaii and otaku internet culture — entire pet and plush-toy accounts exist just to catalogue mofumofu things, and the word has been informally adopted by English-speaking anime fans who now just say 'so mofu' with no translation needed.
